column1: Table of Writers Discussing Connections Between Jesus and Mythic Figures (Mithras, Attis, Dionysus, Apollo, etc.)

column4: Theory/Key Points
Argued Mithras and related cults influenced Christian myth, highlighting dying-and-rising god motifs.
Connected dying-and-rising gods (Osiris, Attis, Adonis, Dionysus) to universal mythic patterns, influencing Christianity.
Drew parallels between Mithras and Jesus, including birth date, savior roles, and ritual similarities.

column3: The Dionysiac Mysteries of the Hellenistic and Roman Age
column4: Compared Dionysus and Osiris, highlighting rebirth and resurrection motifs.
Jesus as a mythic figure, constructed from motifs of dying-and-rising gods like Mithras, Dionysus, Attis.

column3: The Christ Conspiracy, Suns of God
column4: Christianity as a copycat religion, borrowing from Mithras, Dionysus, Krishna, and others.
Details ritual and mythic parallels between Jesus and Dionysus, Osiris, Attis.

column3: Online articles, books by Acharya S
column4: Details similarities between Attis, Mithras, and Jesus: virgin birth, resurrection, sacred meal, etc.
Critically reviews and often refutes alleged parallels between Attis, Mithras, and Jesus.
Claims Jesus’ story borrows motifs from Mithras, Osiris, Adonis, Dionysus, including December 25 birth.
Claims Jesus is the latest in a series of sun deities, paralleling Mithras, Attis, Dionysus, Horus.

column3: On the Historicity of Jesus, blog articles
column4: Argues Jesus fits the "dying-and-rising god" pattern; Christianity borrowed from pagan mystery cults.
Argues Christianity borrowed essential elements from Mithraism and other pagan cults.

column3: "The Truth About Mythological Figures Similar To Jesus"
column4: Discusses similarities and differences between Jesus and Osiris, Dionysus, Attis, etc.
Summarizes mainstream and alternative views on Jesus’ parallels with Mediterranean deities.

column3: Greek Buddha: Pyrrho's Encounter with Early Buddhism, Socrethics, Language Log
column4: Argues Buddha was of Scythian (Saka) origin; Pyrrho's philosophy derived from Buddhist ideas encountered in Bactria; highlights Scythian contributions to Eurasian philosophy and religion, including Zoroastrianism and Buddhism45.
Details the role of Saka/Scythian elites in Khotan and the influence of Zoroastrian and Iranian ideas on Mahayana Buddhism; notes Saka/Scythian transmission of Buddhism along the Silk Road7.

Documents Hellenistic-Buddhist interaction after Alexander’s campaigns; Greco-Bactrian and Indo-Greek kingdoms as conduits for Buddhist, Zoroastrian, and Hellenistic religious exchange; Pyrrho’s skepticism linked to Buddhist meditation5.

column3: "Indo-European religion, Scythian philosophy, and the date of..."
column4: Discusses Scythian roots of Zoroaster, Buddha, and other Eurasian thinkers; posits a shared Scythian philosophical substrate for Zoroastrianism, Buddhism, and Hellenistic thought4.

Describes Indo-Greek syncretism with Buddhism, Zoroastrianism, and Hinduism; Hellenistic artistic influence on Buddhist statuary; Mithraic imagery on Indo-Greek coinage; possible Iranian roots for Maitreya8.
Notes Saka/Scythian connections to Buddhism and Zoroastrianism; Saka elites as Buddhist patrons in Central Asia12.
Discuss comparative links between Zoroastrianism, Buddhism, Hellenism, and Scythian religions; discuss Zunism as a syncretic faith with Scythian, Zoroastrian, and Buddhist elements36.

column2: Dennis AR McDonald (Harvard) publishes "The Gospels and Homer" and "Luke and Virgil" examining Greek influences

Dr. McLean (mentioned in document) analyzes parallels between Dionysian religion and Christianity
